claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to know a bit about your child (or children).

**First, could you tell me:**
1. How old is your child?
2. What is your child's name? (This helps me keep track if you have multiple children)

The age is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please share these details and we'll work through your eligibility together!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and want to check if I'm eligible.

I actually have two children I'm asking about. My youngest is Alex, who's 4 years old. And then there's Blake, who's 14.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you're checking your eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Let me work through each child's situation:

**Alex (age 4)** and **Blake (age 14)** are both under 16, which means they potentially qualify for Child Benefit. 

Now I need to check a few more things for each of them. Let me start with the first check:

**Are either Alex or Blake working full-time?**

(Full-time work means working 24 hours or more per week. This is very rare for children this young, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them works. Alex is only 4, so obviously too young, and Blake doesn't have a job either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Since neither Alex nor Blake works full-time, we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now the next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This would include benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Tax credits

(Again, this is very unlikely for children aged 4 and 14, but I need to confirm they are NOT receiving these benefits.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're just kids - I don't think children that young would be getting those sorts of benefits anyway!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You're absolutely right! It's very unusual for children that young to receive benefits in their own right. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake in an apprenticeship?**

(This is relevant for older teenagers, but since Alex is 4 and Blake is 14, I'm assuming they're not in any apprenticeship programs?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them is in an apprenticeship. Alex is way too young, and Blake is still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since neither child is in an apprenticeship, we can move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status, as this is a key requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status AND are working/studying/have sufficient resources
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad

**Do you normally live in the UK?** Or does one of the other situations apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in England.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for both Alex and Blake?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of these apply:

1. **You live with them** (most common)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying for food, clothes, housing, etc. - at least equal to the Child Benefit amount)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex and Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home. But Alex doesn't live with me - Alex lives elsewhere.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is an important distinction, so I need to understand each child's situation separately.

**For Blake (who lives with you):** Since Blake lives with you, you are responsible for Blake. ✓

**For Alex (who doesn't live with you):** I need to ask - **do you contribute to Alex's upkeep?**

This could include:
- Paying for their food, clothes, housing, or other needs
- Making maintenance payments under a court order or agreement
- Providing support that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit amount (currently around £25 per week for the eldest child, £16.95 for additional children)

Do you contribute financially to Alex's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I help out with Alex. I contribute towards things like clothes and other bits they need.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know! To confirm you meet the responsibility requirement for Alex, I need to check:

**Do your contributions for Alex's care (clothes and other items) amount to at least the same value as the Child Benefit payment?**

For context, Child Benefit is currently:
- Around **£25.60 per week** for the eldest/only child
- Around **£16.95 per week** for additional children

Your contributions can be:
- Weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments
- Money or items provided directly (like clothes, food, etc.)

**Would you say your contributions for Alex equal or exceed approximately £25 per week (or around £100+ per month)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you contribute £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, that's well above the Child Benefit amount, so you are considered responsible for Alex as well. ✓

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check an important question about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent or guardian where they live
- Blake's other parent
- Any other person who might be responsible for them

This matters because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake - they live with me.

For Alex, nobody else is claiming it either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake, we can continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check where the children are living and if any special circumstances apply:

**For both Alex and Blake, are either of them:**
1. Currently in hospital?
2. In local authority care (e.g., children's home or care home)?
3. Being fostered?
4. Or are they living in normal circumstances?

Please let me know the situation for each child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both just living in normal circumstances. Blake is at home with me, and Alex lives with their other parent. Neither of them is in hospital, care, or being fostered or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming that both children are in normal living circumstances.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question:

**Is the local council paying towards either Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance costs?**

(This would be payments specifically from the local authority for their living costs - not standard things like state schools or healthcare.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's the final check I needed to make.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit

**You can claim Child Benefit for BOTH Alex and Blake!**

### Summary for Each Child:

**✓ Alex (age 4) - ELIGIBLE**
- Under 16 and meets all qualifying criteria
- You are responsible for Alex through your £50 weekly contributions
- No one else is claiming for Alex
- Living in normal circumstances

**✓ Blake (age 14) - ELIGIBLE**
- Under 16 and meets all qualifying criteria  
- You are responsible for Blake who lives with you
- No one else is claiming for Blake
- Living in normal circumstances

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
   - Online: Visit [Gov.uk Child Benefit claim page](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)
   - By post: Complete form CH2

2. **You'll need to provide:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Each child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
   - Proof of identity for each child

3. **Payment amounts:**
   - **First child (eldest):** Around £25.60 per week
   - **Second child:** Around £16.95 per week
   - Payments are typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

4. **Important to know:**
   - If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**
   - You can still claim and choose not to receive payments if you want to protect your National Insurance credits

Would you like any more information about how to apply or the High Income Child Benefit Charge?

reasonings: Alex (age 4) is eligible because: (1) Alex is under 16 years old, (2) Alex is not working full-time (24+ hours per week), (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK and meets residency requirements, (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living in normal circumstances (not in hospital, care, or fostering), and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 14) is eligible because: (1) Blake is under 16 years old, (2) Blake is not working full-time (24+ hours per week), (3) Blake is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Blake is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK and meets residency requirements, (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake as Blake lives with them, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living in normal circumstances (not in hospital, care, or fostering), and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children. Both Alex (age 4) and Blake (age 14) are under 16 and meet all qualifying criteria. The claimant meets UK residency requirements and is responsible for both children - Blake lives with them, and the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and neither child is in hospital, care, or fostering. The local council is not paying for either child's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied for both children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 4) is eligible because: (1) Alex is under 16 years old, (2) Alex is not working full-time (24+ hours per week), (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK and meets residency requirements, (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living in normal circumstances (not in hospital, care, or fostering), and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 14) is eligible because: (1) Blake is under 16 years old, (2) Blake is not working full-time (24+ hours per week), (3) Blake is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Blake is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK and meets residency requirements, (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake as Blake lives with them, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living in normal circumstances (not in hospital, care, or fostering), and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children. Both Alex (age 4) and Blake (age 14) are under 16 and meet all qualifying criteria. The claimant meets UK residency requirements and is responsible for both children - Blake lives with them, and the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and neither child is in hospital, care, or fostering. The local council is not paying for either child's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied for both children.
